metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Architecture constraints 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet to say the sanitizer is TO BE BUILT (not already implemented): remove the 'IMPLEMENTED + TESTED' claim and instead reference the child story that will build it, so no implementer assumes it already exists.
findings.finding: The Architecture constraints section asserts 'This is an IMPLEMENTED + TESTED sanitizer (a planted secret is asserted scrubbed), not documentation-only' for the diagnostic sanitizer, but no such implementation exists in the codebase (zero matches for secret-scrubbing in src/rebar/llm, src/rebar/_logging.py has no redaction logic, and no planted-secret test exists). The plan contradicts itself: the AC section correctly says 'implemented AND tested', implying it must be built, but the constraints section falsely presents it as already-existing infrastructure. Child story authors reading the constraints section may skip building the sanitizer, leading to secrets/API-keys appearing verbatim in logs, CLI stderr, session logs, and verdict payloads.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC bullet 10
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'This is an IMPLEMENTED + TESTED sanitizer (a planted secret is asserted scrubbed), not documentation-only' to 'This MUST BE implemented and tested (a planted secret asserted scrubbed before any sink); the implementation is owned by [child story X], not documentation-only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Architecture constraints section to read 'TO BE implemented and tested' (removing the false present-tense 'IMPLEMENTED + TESTED' claim) and assign the sanitizer implementation explicitly to a named child story, so no author mistakes it for existing infrastructure.
findings.finding: The diagnostic sanitizer is described in the Architecture constraints as 'This is an IMPLEMENTED + TESTED sanitizer (a planted secret is asserted scrubbed), not documentation-only' — but no such implementation exists in the codebase (zero matches for any secret-scrubbing/redaction logic in src/rebar/llm or src/rebar/_logging.py). The AC section correctly lists it as a future deliverable. This contradicts itself: child story authors reading the constraints section may skip building the sanitizer, believing it already exists.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC bullet 10
findings.suggested_fix: Change the constraints bullet to: 'The diagnostic sanitizer is a DELIVERABLE (not pre-existing infrastructure): it must be IMPLEMENTED and tested — a planted secret asserted scrubbed before any sink — as part of the gate-wiring child story (or a dedicated child). Its contract is also recorded in an ADR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `resolution_class` (with its allowed enum values) and `resolution_diagnostic` to the `coverage` sub-schema (or as top-level verdict properties) in plan_review_verdict.schema.json, code_review_verdict.schema.json, and completion_verdict.schema.json — or explicitly state an alternative schema location — so the exit-11 bifurcation rule has a concrete, typed schema anchor.
findings.finding: The plan says exit-11 vs INDETERMINATE-exit-2 is 'bifurcated by the resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage,' but neither the plan_review_verdict schema nor the code_review_verdict schema nor the completion_verdict schema defines a `resolution_class` property. The `coverage` field is `additionalProperties: true` in all three schemas with no sub-properties specified, leaving the key name, type, and allowed values undefined at the schema layer.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; AC bullet 4; src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json coverage property; src/rebar/schemas/code_review_verdict.schema.json coverage property; src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to 30ff (or the failure.py module spec) asserting the LLMOutcome constructor is usable for non-exception construction with the same field guarantees, and add a test that 978d's refusal-as-content call site produces a correctly formed LLMOutcome.
findings.finding: Child 30ff's acceptance criteria cover classify_llm_failure only for the exception path, but child 978d's refusal-as-content detection explicitly calls 30ff's LLMOutcome constructor on a non-exception (result-validation) path. 30ff's AC has no line asserting the LLMOutcome value-object constructor is usable for non-exception construction, leaving the interface contract between 978d and 30ff's failure.py module partially underspecified.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc (Refusal-as-content protocol section); child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b (Acceptance Criteria + Scope)
findings.suggested_fix: In 30ff's AC, add: 'LLMOutcome can be constructed directly (not only via classify_llm_failure) with a resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic; a test covers the direct-construction path as consumed by story 978d's refusal-as-content route.' Document the full public API surface of failure.py (not just the classify_llm_failure entry point) in the inter-child API boundary note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet to 2eca (or a dedicated integration test) that asserts all six required config keys exist with their specified default values in the final config schema after wave-3 reconciliation.
findings.finding: AC12 (config keys with concrete defaults) is covered in aggregate by 95a5 (retry config) and fd1d (timeout + step-cap config), but no single child AC asserts that ALL four config groups (retry, request timeout, tool timeout, step caps) are present and correct in the final integrated config.py / _config_schema.py. 2eca is the config-namespace integrator but its AC only says 'consolidated docs updated' — it does not include a test or lint that verifies every required default key exists with the specified value.
findings.location: Parent AC12; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 AC (all bullets); child 95a5 Scope; child fd1d Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema-validation test (e.g., `test_config_defaults.py`) that instantiates a default config and asserts each of the six keys (llm_retry_max_attempts=4, llm_retry_max_wait_s=60, llm_request_timeout_s=600, llm_tool_timeout_s=120, llm_agentic_request_limit=60, llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit=200) is present with the exact parent-specified default. Assign ownership to 2eca's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an integration test (owned by 2eca as wave-3 integrator, or explicitly delegated to 95a5+fd1d) that exercises the combined httpx.AsyncClient with both AsyncTenacityTransport and httpx.Timeout active simultaneously, asserting that a transient 429 retries correctly and a genuine hung request eventually trips the timeout without the retry loop swallowing it.
findings.finding: Child 95a5 (wave 2) constructs the shared httpx.AsyncClient that carries the AsyncTenacityTransport, and child fd1d (wave 2, parallel) contributes the httpx.Timeout into that same client. No child AC asserts that the *combined* client — with both the transport and the timeout set simultaneously — is integration-tested. The two settings interact (a tenacity retry loop around a request that hits the read timeout produces a different error path than a retry loop alone), but the joint behavior is only tested by inference from the two separate per-child tests.
findings.location: child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b (shared client ownership); child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4 (shared-client coordination section); child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (wave-3 integration)
findings.suggested_fix: In 95a5's spike or fd1d's timeout probe, add a combined scenario: mock transport returns 429 (Retry-After=1s) then sleeps past the read timeout on the retry attempt — assert the ReadTimeout is classified as WAIT_AND_RETRY and does not silently succeed. Assign joint ownership to 95a5+fd1d with 2eca confirming in wave-3.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to 95a5's scope or AC an explicit consumer-impact analysis for the comment-tool dedupe key change, confirming backward compatibility (or naming a migration) for MCP callers and the Gerrit/Jira comment backends.
findings.finding: Child 95a5 makes the comment tool idempotent via a client-generated dedupe key, which modifies the tool's observable behavior (the posted message shape or API call parameters). No sibling addresses how existing MCP callers or other consumers of the rebar comment tool should handle or are unaffected by the new dedupe key field in the request/response.
findings.location: child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b (Scope; Acceptance Criteria)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to 95a5's AC: 'The dedupe key is added as an optional request header or idempotency-key field that the comment backend ignores if not recognized; existing consumers observing the raw HTTP request are enumerated and confirmed compatible.' If the backend doesn't support idempotency keys natively, document the dedupe strategy (client-side deduplication via a seen-keys cache instead).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a public documentation citation (pydantic-ai changelog URL or PyPI `Provides-Extra` entry for the specific version that introduced `[retries]` + `AsyncTenacityTransport`) and tighten the `pydantic-ai-slim` lower-bound pin to the first version that ships this extra.
findings.finding: The plan's central capability assertion — that `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is a real package extra shipping `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` — is grounded solely by a local runtime introspection command (`importlib.metadata` / `importlib.util.find_spec`), not by a public changelog URL, release notes, or PyPI metadata citation. Three prior review passes flagged this as nonexistent; the plan dismisses them as 'false-positive from a code-blind reviewer' without providing a durable external reference. The `pydantic-ai-slim` pin is `>=1,<2` (loose), meaning older 1.x installs may not have this extra, silently breaking the transport-retry layer across environments.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Cite the specific pydantic-ai release (e.g., `>=1.107.0,<2`) in which `[retries]` was introduced, link to the relevant changelog entry or GitHub commit, and update pyproject.toml's lower bound accordingly so the loose `>=1` pin cannot resolve to a pre-extra version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Record the actual observed output of the verification probe (`python -c "import importlib.metadata as m; print('retries' in m.metadata('pydantic-ai-slim').get_all('Provides-Extra'))"` AND `importlib.util.find_spec('pydantic_ai.retries')` AND `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport, wait_retry_after`) as a committed artifact in the spike (story 95a5), and block all dependent stories on that artifact.
findings.finding: `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `wait_retry_after` are asserted to exist in pydantic-ai-slim 1.107.0, but this is Unverified from the codebase: no repo code imports or uses these symbols, `pyproject.toml` does not declare the `[retries]` extra, and the plan's verification evidence is an unexecuted shell command (not observed output). Three prior review passes independently flagged these as nonexistent. The spike (story 95a5) is the stated de-risking mechanism, but no recorded spike output appears in the repo.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `spike-output.md` or equivalent committed file to the 95a5 spike that records the exact import paths, class signatures, and a minimal working example of `AsyncTenacityTransport` injected into `AnthropicProvider(http_client=...)` and exercised under `agent.run_sync()`. Gate the gate-wiring story's start on this artifact being present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Include in spike 95a5 an empirically observed constructor call `AnthropicProvider(http_client=<AsyncTenacityTransport instance>)` with confirmation that a simulated 429→200 round-trip actually retries at the transport layer (not just that the parameter is accepted).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `AnthropicProvider.__init__` accepts `http_client=` and `anthropic_client=` parameters, enabling transport injection, but the current codebase uses `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` only — the `http_client=` parameter is never exercised in any existing code path, making this a new, unverified integration surface on an already-used class.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; runner.py:237-240

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit intra-wave ordering note: 9a82 must integrate its capability check AFTER 95a5's single-construction-path refactor lands (or be designed to attach at a stable hook point that 95a5 exports), and add a joint test that the capability check fires on the refactored path.
findings.finding: Child 9a82's 'construction-time capability assertion at model construction' implicitly depends on the single unified Anthropic model construction path that child 95a5 refactors in the same wave 2. If 9a82 is developed against the pre-refactored construction path (before 95a5 lands), its capability check may fire on the wrong construction site and fail to intercept the post-refactored path — with no intra-wave ordering constraint or AC guard to catch this.
findings.location: child 9a82-3109-29b5-471d (What; Scope); child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b (What — 'refactor to a SINGLE Anthropic-model construction'); 2eca wave ordering
findings.suggested_fix: Define a named hook in 95a5's refactored construction (e.g., a `_build_anthropic_model()` factory function) that 9a82's capability check wraps or extends. This makes the dependency explicit and ensures 9a82 is implemented against the correct post-refactor target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all 7 child stories (by ID and title) in the plan body or add a cross-reference table so the first AC is verifiable without consulting an external tracker.
findings.finding: The AC's first criterion — 'All 7 child stories are delivered' — references a specific count (7) that has no enumeration or derivable basis anywhere in the plan body. Only one child story (95a5, the transport-injection spike) is named. An implementer or reviewer cannot verify '7' from this plan alone, making the AC unverifiable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: 'All 7 child stories are delivered, each passing its own acceptance criteria and CI'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Child stories' section listing all 7 story IDs and one-line summaries, matching the merge-order sequence already described (0→1→{2,3,4,5}→6 implies at least 7 items).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement in the plan body (Architecture Constraints or the gate-wiring story description) that the transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture decision will be recorded as an ADR, consistent with the other three.
findings.finding: The fourth ADR — 'transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture' — appears only in the AC list but is never described, named, or flagged as an ADR deliverable in the plan body. The other three ADRs are explicitly called out ("deferred to an ADR", "recorded as an ADR", "contract is ALSO recorded as an ADR") in the Architecture Constraints section, creating an orphan AC criterion with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — ADR bullet: '...and the transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture'; contrast with Architecture Constraints section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Architecture Constraints section, after the spike description (story 95a5), add: 'The side-effect-safety rationale (retries below the agent graph prevent tool re-execution) is recorded as an ADR, single owner: the gate-wiring story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology throughout the plan: pick one canonical name for both the field and the concept (e.g., use `resolution_class` / `ResolutionClass` consistently, or rename the field to `resolution_disposition` and update all references).
findings.finding: Terminology drift between `resolution_class` (the field name used in Architecture Constraints) and 'resolution-disposition' (the concept name used in the main body and AC). The same entity is described with two different names across sections, which could cause implementers to create mismatched field names or documentation.
findings.location: Architecture Constraints — 'Exit-code signal' bullet ('resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage') vs. main body ('closed resolution-disposition set') vs. AC ('closed resolution-disposition enum')
findings.suggested_fix: Choose `resolution_class` as the canonical term (it appears in the most precise context — the architectural constraint) and update all other occurrences ('resolution-disposition set/enum') to match, or vice versa — just be consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the `max_retries=0` change (e.g., a `verify.llm_use_sdk_retries` escape-hatch flag that restores `max_retries=2` and bypasses the transport layer, or a documented procedure to revert the dependency + config), and state it in the Architecture constraints section alongside the exit-code rollback.
findings.finding: The behavioral change from SDK-default 2 retries to `max_retries=0` (retries moved entirely to the transport layer) has no explicit rollback path. The startup guard 'fails fast' if transport retry is misconfigured, but leaves the system with 0 retries — strictly worse than the current baseline of 2 — with no stated mechanism to revert to the old behavior.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC line: 'Transport retry + `max_retries=0` apply on EVERY Anthropic model-construction path … with a startup guard that fails fast rather than silently regressing from today's 2 retries to 0'
findings.suggested_fix: Document a two-step rollback: (1) set `verify.llm_retry_max_attempts=0` to disable transport retries, AND restore `max_retries=2` on the Anthropic client constructor (the pre-epic default) as a paired config path, so operators can return to exactly the pre-epic behavior without a code change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address verdict schema backward-compatibility: either (a) declare the verdict schema as internal-only (no external consumers), (b) provide an expand-contract sequence (add new fields, deprecate old, then remove), or (c) state the rollback procedure for schema changes; and add a T7 requirement for a schema changelog or ADR note covering the structural additions.
findings.finding: The verdict schema modifications are called out as happening ('the verdict schemas … are touched by multiple children; the gate-wiring story is … single-source-of-truth for … verdict-schema edits') but the plan contains no explicit discussion of whether existing consumers of the verdict JSON (e.g., CI parsers, downstream tooling) break, and no expand-contract sequence or rollback for the schema changes.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Shared-file integration'; AC (no explicit verdict-schema-doc or compat line)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation task (in the gate-wiring story or as a standalone AC line) to update install/setup docs and the CHANGELOG to explain the `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` / `tenacity` addition, its rationale, and how to verify correct installation.
findings.finding: The plan adds `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as a hard new dependency (pulling in `tenacity`) but does not call out updating installation documentation, setup guides, or any `requirements.txt`/`pyproject.toml` commentary that explains the new extra. Operators upgrading rebar will see a new transitive dependency with no documented rationale in install-facing docs.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC line: '`pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the gate-wiring story's AC: 'Install docs and CHANGELOG updated to note `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as a new required extra, with the reproducible verification command from the Architecture constraints section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal timeout-under-run_sync probe (part of 95a5 or a sibling spike) that empirically verifies a deliberately-stalled httpx request is interrupted within the configured read timeout when called through `agent.run_sync()`, and that a stalled tool call is interrupted by the per-tool timeout — confirming the silent-hang backstop actually fires before building dependent gate-wiring logic on it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that per-request read timeouts and per-tool timeouts will correctly interrupt a stalled `agent.run_sync()` call (the silent-hang backstop), but the 95a5 spike only validates retry injection, not timeout firing. Under `run_sync`, pydantic-ai manages an internal event loop and drives `AsyncAnthropic`; whether an httpx read timeout propagates correctly through that internal loop to surface as a catchable exception — rather than silently hanging or raising from within a non-surfacing coroutine — is an empirically non-trivial question that the plan does not propose to probe.
findings.location: Architecture constraints / Success Criteria ('a genuinely stalled run is caught by the per-request timeout, the per-tool timeout') / AC ('Agentic ops use run_sync-compatible liveness')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the 95a5 spike with a second scenario: inject a mock transport that hangs indefinitely (no response), configure a short read timeout (e.g., 2s), call `agent.run_sync()`, and assert the call raises a timeout exception within ~3s. Repeat for a mock tool that sleeps forever with a short per-tool timeout. This confirms the liveness mechanism before the gate-wiring story relies on it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the extension strategy for the resolution-disposition enum — at minimum: assert that all switch-sites use a default/fallthrough branch (treating unknown values as NEEDS_INVESTIGATION), note whether enum values are serialized into persistent artifacts (session logs, verdict files) and if so the backward-compat rule, and add a note to the ADR or CLAUDE.md that adding a value requires auditing all switch-sites.
findings.finding: The closed resolution-disposition enum (8 values: WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) is a business rule expected to evolve as new providers, failure modes, or routing needs emerge, but the plan includes no documented strategy for how the enum is extended (versioning, backward-compat for persisted verdicts/session-logs that serialize disposition values, or a changelog process).
findings.location: Architecture constraints ('Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set') / AC ('Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum')
findings.suggested_fix: In the classifier ADR (owned by gate-wiring story): add a section 'Extensibility' stating (1) new values MUST be added with a corresponding default-branch audit, (2) if disposition is serialized, unknown string values MUST deserialize to NEEDS_INVESTIGATION, and (3) the binary retry-or-not mapping is the sole behavior-critical contract — all other routing is advisory and consumers must handle unknown values gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate every call site that catches or inspects `LLMUnavailableError` (or any type it wraps) outside the three gate commands and runner.py, confirm each is updated or explicitly noted as unaffected, and add this audit to the gate-wiring story's definition of done.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the single opaque `LLMUnavailableError` with a richer error/exception surface (disposition enum, new exit 11, new guard on signing) but does not enumerate or acknowledge any call sites outside the gate layer that catch or inspect `LLMUnavailableError` directly. If such callers exist, their behavior silently changes.
findings.location: Context section ('a 429 is indistinguishable… collapses into one opaque LLMUnavailableError at src/rebar/llm/runner.py:349-361') and Architecture constraints ('Exit code 11 amends a FROZEN contract')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a grep/AST audit step in the gate-wiring story: `grep -rn 'LLMUnavailableError' src/ tests/` and document each hit's disposition (unchanged / updated / intentionally left as-is with rationale). If the exception type is being preserved as a re-raised wrapper, say so explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact integration point(s) for the 'never certify degraded result' structural guard: explicitly name whether the resolution_class→no-sign check is enforced at the verdict-production layer (before returning), at each signing wrapper (plan_review/__init__.py and transition_close.py), or both — and confirm both plan-review attestation and close-gate signing paths are covered.
findings.finding: The 'never certify a degraded result' structural guard is described as a 'structural guard on signing' but the plan does not specify the exact integration point — whether the check lives at the verdict-production layer (before returning the result), at the signing wrapper in plan_review/__init__.py:489, or at the close gate in _commands/transition_close.py:199. The existing code already has multiple signing paths with separate guard logic (certifiable=False for force-closed children, plan_review/__init__.py attestation signing). Without a precise integration point, the gate-wiring story (the named integrator) cannot be certain it has covered all signing paths, creating a risk that a WAIT_AND_RETRY resolution_class result could reach one of the signing paths and be incorrectly signed.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Never certify a degraded result' bullet; AC bullet 6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document how the 8-value resolution-disposition enum maps to (or extends beyond) the Anthropic SDK's existing exception hierarchy and pydantic-ai's `ModelRetry`/`UnexpectedModelBehavior` taxonomy, justifying each class that is novel vs. each that merely re-labels an existing exception.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom 8-value resolution-disposition taxonomy (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) without surveying existing, established error classification schemes that the OSS community already uses for LLM failures — specifically the Anthropic SDK's own exception hierarchy (`RateLimitError`, `APIStatusError`, `AuthenticationError`, `BadRequestError`, `InternalServerError`, etc.) and the pydantic-ai `ModelRetry` / `UnexpectedModelBehavior` taxonomy. These established hierarchies already encode the retry-or-not decision and the routing hint the plan's enum is meant to supply.
findings.location: Settled design decisions — 'Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set'; AC: 'Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend spike 95a5 to explicitly verify the side-effect-safety claim: inject a write-tracking test tool, trigger a simulated 429 mid-agentic-run, and confirm the tool is called exactly once across the retry, with the confirmed result recorded as a committed artifact.
findings.finding: The plan claims transport-layer retries under `agent.run_sync()` are safe from tool-side-effect duplication because retries happen 'below the agent graph,' but this relies on an unverified assumption about pydantic-ai 1.107.0's internal async dispatch: specifically, that a mid-request 429 at the httpx layer occurs after the model request is sent but before any tool-call response is processed, and that the agent's state machine does not replay tool calls on retry. This is the architecturally critical side-effect-safety property, and it is deferred to the spike.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'No streaming'; AC bullet 'Retry is owned at exactly one layer'; Success Criteria bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `pyproject.toml` change for the dependency addition: confirm whether `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2` becomes `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]>=1,<2` (or equivalent), and that `tenacity` is transitively satisfied.
findings.finding: The plan requires adding `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as a dependency but does not specify how it combines with the existing `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]` declaration in `pyproject.toml`. It is unclear whether the plan intends `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]` (combined extras on one package spec) or two separate requirements, and which extra group (`[agents]` vs a new one) receives it.
findings.location: AC — 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'; pyproject.toml line 108
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `[agents]` extra entry from `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2` to `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]>=1,<2`, and add `tenacity` explicitly as a direct dependency if `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` does not guarantee a floor version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision table mapping observable conditions (HTTP status, exception type/message pattern) to each of the 8 resolution-disposition enum members, and explicitly enumerate which members are 'retryable' (triggering exit 11) vs 'non-retryable' (remaining exit 2), co-located with the enum definition in the plan or the classifier child story.
findings.finding: The closed resolution-disposition enum (8 values: WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) is referenced throughout the plan but no decision table maps observable conditions (HTTP status codes, exception types, error message patterns) to specific enum members. The plan says only that the classifier is 'total (unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) and never itself raises' — but the routing logic for the other 7 members is entirely absent.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Classify what survives retries' bullet; AC bullet 3 ('Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum + verbatim diagnostic')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table like: 429/529 + Retry-After present → WAIT_AND_RETRY; 529 without Retry-After → RETRY_NOW; 5xx → RETRY_NOW; 401/403 → CHANGE_SETTINGS; context-length exceeded → CHANGE_INPUT; model deprecated/unavailable → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL; quota exhausted (non-transient) → INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS; tool loop exceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN; all other/unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION. Retryable set = {WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW} → exit 11.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'structured refusal-as-content' detection rule: specify the textual/structural patterns (e.g., a minimum set of refusal phrases to match in the verdict `summary` or `findings[].finding`), the threshold, which schema fields are checked, and which resolution_class the detection assigns — distinguishing it clearly from the existing `check_stop_reason()` stop_reason-based detection.
findings.finding: The 'structured refusal-as-content detection' named as a new silent-success layer item is never defined: no decision rule specifies what textual patterns or structural signatures constitute a 'refusal returned as content' (as distinct from the already-implemented stop_reason-based `check_stop_reason()` which catches `refusal`/`content_filter` stop reasons). The plan presents this as additive but provides no pattern vocabulary, threshold, or routing rule.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Silent-success layer' bullet ('structured refusal-as-content detection'); Success Criteria bullet 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'faulty-output-in-reask' protocol: specify what content is injected into the reask (the faulty raw output? a structured diff from the expected schema?), where in the conversation it is placed, how it differs from the current validation-error retry, and whether it applies to the pydantic-ai OUTPUT_RETRIES path or a new outer loop.
findings.finding: The 'faulty-output-in-reask' output self-heal tweak is named as an additive change on top of the existing structured-output retry path, but no protocol is defined: the plan does not specify what 'faulty output' is appended to the reask, in what format, at what position in the conversation, or how this differs from the current bounded retry that already feeds the validation error back (layer 4 of structured.py). Without a schema for the reask message, implementers cannot build this consistently.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Silent-success layer' bullet ('faulty-output-in-reask'); Success Criteria bullet 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in a879's AC that ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False is scoped per-test (e.g., via an autouse fixture with teardown or a pytest monkeypatch) to prevent flag leakage into other test modules.
findings.finding: Child a879 guards its tests with `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` but does not specify whether this flag is set at module/session level or per-test. If set globally at module import time or as a session-scoped pytest fixture, it can bleed into other test modules in the same pytest session and break any existing tests that depend on live or stubbed model requests outside the failure-matrix suite.
findings.location: child a879-3b64-1be9-4035 (Scope section; What section)
findings.suggested_fix: Use a pytest autouse fixture within the a879 test file: `@pytest.fixture(autouse=True) def no_model_requests(monkeypatch): monkeypatch.setattr(pydantic_ai.models, 'ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS', False)` — this restores the original value after each test via monkeypatch's teardown.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the config key namespace contract (all six key names and types) in 2eca's What section and share it with 95a5 and fd1d BEFORE wave-2 implementation begins, so per-child tests pin the final agreed names from the start.
findings.finding: Children 95a5 and fd1d both modify config.py and _config_schema.py in wave 2 (parallel). 2eca is the wave-3 integrator and 'single-source-of-truth for the config namespace,' but each child's per-child tests assert specific config key names and defaults. If 2eca's wave-3 reconciliation renames, merges, or restructures any config key, the per-child tests that assert the original names would break — creating a catch-22 where per-child green tests conflict with the integrated config namespace.
findings.location: child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b (Scope; Files: config.py, _config_schema.py); child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4 (Scope; Files: config.py, _config_schema.py); child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (Integration ownership; config namespace)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config-key name table to 2eca's What section (or a shared design note) listing all six keys with their final names, types, and defaults. Require 95a5 and fd1d to use exactly these names in their implementation and tests. 2eca's wave-3 role becomes validation rather than renaming.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
